---
title: "Redesigning Global Fossil CO2 Emissions Visualization"
subtitle: "CSC3107 Information Visualization -- Team SlateBlue"
author: "Team SlateBlue"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 7
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## 1. Introduction

This project redesigns a [stacked area chart of global fossil CO2 emissions](https://globalcarbonbudget.org/gcb-2024/) published by Carbon Brief using data from the Global Carbon Project (GCB 2024). The original chart shows annual fossil CO2 emissions from 1959--2024 broken down by China, India, the United States, the European Union, and the Rest of the World.

While the chart effectively communicates the overall upward trend, the stacked area format makes it nearly impossible to compare individual regions---only China (the bottom layer) is directly readable. Our redesign addresses this and other issues identified in our critical analysis.

## 2. Design Brief

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
## Design Brief

This redesign targets general-public readers of climate news who want to understand which countries drive CO2 emissions growth.
The goal is to compare both **total emissions** and **per-capita emissions** across regions so readers can assess national responsibility on a fair basis.

**Main revisions:**

- **Sparkline summary table** for at-a-glance comparison of trends, latest values, and % change across all regions
- **Faceted line charts** (small multiples) to replace the stacked area chart, giving each region a shared zero baseline
- **Per-capita panel** alongside total emissions to provide population-adjusted context
- **Direct labels** with `ggrepel` to eliminate legend look-up
- **Event annotations** (Paris Agreement, COVID-19 dip) to connect trends to real-world context
- **% share representation** instead of a separate "Rest of World" area to reduce visual clutter
- **Colorblind-friendly palette** (Okabe--Ito) for accessibility
:::

## 3. Original Visualization

The original visualization is a stacked area chart from Carbon Brief / Global Carbon Project:

![Original visualization: Global CO2 emissions from fossil fuels by region, 1959--2024 (Source: Carbon Brief / Global Carbon Project)](original_visualization/original_chart.png){width=80%}

**Source:** Carbon Brief, "Global Carbon Budget 2024", [globalcarbonbudget.org/gcb-2024/](https://globalcarbonbudget.org/gcb-2024/)

### Critique of Original

**Strengths:**

- Long 65-year timespan reveals structural shifts (China's post-2000 surge, EU decline, COVID-19 dip)
- Zero-based y-axis is correct for absolute quantities
- Successfully conveys the core message of record-high global emissions

**Weaknesses:**

- Stacked area chart requires mental subtraction to read any region except the bottom layer (China)
- Similar blue shades reduce separability between regions, especially for colorblind viewers
- No per-capita context misleads readers about individual-level responsibility
- Legend order does not match visual stacking order
- No annotations for key events (Paris Agreement 2015, COVID-19 2020)

## 4. Data Preparation

### 4.1 Data Source

The dataset is **National Fossil Carbon Emissions 2024 v1.0** from the Global Carbon Project.

- **Source:** [globalcarbonbudget.org/carbonbudget](https://globalcarbonbudget.org/carbonbudget)
- **File:** `National_Fossil_Carbon_Emissions_2024v1.0-1.xlsx`
- **Sheet used:** "Territorial Emissions"
- **Units:** Million tonnes of carbon per year (MtC/yr), converted to GtCO2 by multiplying by 3.664 / 1000
- **Coverage:** Country-level emissions from 1850--2023 (~250 countries)

Population data for per-capita calculations is sourced from the **UN World Population Prospects** ([population.un.org/wpp](https://population.un.org/wpp/downloads)).

### 4.2 Data Import and Cleaning

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)
library(gtExtras)
```

```{r}
#| label: import-data
emissions_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/raw/National_Fossil_Carbon_Emissions_2024v1.0-1.xlsx",
  sheet = "Territorial Emissions",
  skip = 11
)

head(emissions_raw[, 1:6])
```

```{r}
#| label: clean-data
# The first column is the year, remaining columns are countries
# Row 1 has uppercase names, row 2 has proper names -- we skipped headers so
# the first real row of data should start with year 1850

# Rename the year column
names(emissions_raw)[1] <- "year"

# Remove any header/description rows (keep only numeric years)
emissions_clean <- emissions_raw |>
  filter(!is.na(year)) |>
  mutate(year = as.numeric(year)) |>
  filter(!is.na(year))

# EU27 member states (as listed in the Regions sheet of the dataset)
eu27_countries <- c(
  "Austria",
  "Belgium",
  "Bulgaria",
  "Croatia",
  "Cyprus",
  "Czechia",
  "Denmark",
  "Estonia",
  "Finland",
  "France",
  "Germany",
  "Greece",
  "Hungary",
  "Ireland",
  "Italy",
  "Latvia",
  "Lithuania",
  "Luxembourg",
  "Malta",
  "Netherlands",
  "Poland",
  "Portugal",
  "Romania",
  "Slovakia",
  "Slovenia",
  "Spain",
  "Sweden"
)

# Find matching column names (case-insensitive)
col_names <- names(emissions_clean)

find_col <- function(pattern) {
  idx <- grep(pattern, col_names, ignore.case = TRUE)
  if (length(idx) > 0) col_names[idx[1]] else NA
}

china_col <- find_col("^China$")
india_col <- find_col("^India$")
usa_col <- find_col("^USA$")
world_col <- find_col("^World$")

# Find EU27 columns
eu27_cols <- sapply(eu27_countries, function(country) {
  idx <- grep(paste0("^", country, "$"), col_names, ignore.case = TRUE)
  if (length(idx) > 0) col_names[idx[1]] else NA
})
eu27_cols <- eu27_cols[!is.na(eu27_cols)]

cat("China column:", china_col, "\n")
cat("India column:", india_col, "\n")
cat("USA column:", usa_col, "\n")
cat("World column:", world_col, "\n")
cat("EU27 columns found:", length(eu27_cols), "of 27\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: aggregate-regions
# Convert MtC/yr to GtCO2: multiply by 3.664 / 1000
MTC_TO_GTCO2 <- 3.664 / 1000

emissions_regions <- emissions_clean |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    china_gtco2 = ifelse(
      !is.na(.data[[china_col]]),
      as.numeric(.data[[china_col]]) * MTC_TO_GTCO2,
      0
    ),
    india_gtco2 = ifelse(
      !is.na(.data[[india_col]]),
      as.numeric(.data[[india_col]]) * MTC_TO_GTCO2,
      0
    ),
    usa_gtco2 = ifelse(
      !is.na(.data[[usa_col]]),
      as.numeric(.data[[usa_col]]) * MTC_TO_GTCO2,
      0
    ),
    world_gtco2 = ifelse(
      !is.na(.data[[world_col]]),
      as.numeric(.data[[world_col]]) * MTC_TO_GTCO2,
      0
    ),
    eu27_gtco2 = sum(
      across(all_of(eu27_cols), ~ ifelse(is.na(.x), 0, as.numeric(.x))),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ) *
      MTC_TO_GTCO2
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    rest_of_world_gtco2 = world_gtco2 -
      china_gtco2 -
      india_gtco2 -
      usa_gtco2 -
      eu27_gtco2
  ) |>
  select(
    year,
    china_gtco2,
    india_gtco2,
    usa_gtco2,
    eu27_gtco2,
    rest_of_world_gtco2,
    world_gtco2
  ) |>
  filter(year >= 1959, year <= 2023)

head(emissions_regions)
```

```{r}
#| label: pivot-long
emissions_long <- emissions_regions |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      china_gtco2,
      india_gtco2,
      usa_gtco2,
      eu27_gtco2,
      rest_of_world_gtco2
    ),
    names_to = "region",
    values_to = "emissions_gtco2"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    region = case_match(
      region,
      "china_gtco2" ~ "China",
      "india_gtco2" ~ "India",
      "usa_gtco2" ~ "United States",
      "eu27_gtco2" ~ "European Union",
      "rest_of_world_gtco2" ~ "Rest of World"
    ),
    region = factor(
      region,
      levels = c(
        "China",
        "United States",
        "European Union",
        "India",
        "Rest of World"
      )
    )
  )

# Compute % share of global total
emissions_long <- emissions_long |>
  mutate(pct_share = emissions_gtco2 / world_gtco2 * 100)
```

```{r}
#| label: compute-pct-change
# Year-over-year % change for each region
emissions_long <- emissions_long |>
  group_by(region) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  mutate(
    pct_change = (emissions_gtco2 - lag(emissions_gtco2)) /
      lag(emissions_gtco2) *
      100
  ) |>
  ungroup()
```

```{r}
#| label: save-cleaned-data
write_csv(emissions_long, "data/cleaned/emissions_by_region.csv")
write_csv(emissions_regions, "data/cleaned/emissions_wide.csv")
```

**Data cleaning steps performed:**

1. Imported the "Territorial Emissions" sheet, skipping 11 header/metadata rows
2. Identified country columns for China (Mainland), India, United States, and EU27 member states
3. Converted units from MtC/yr to GtCO2 (multiply by 3.664 / 1000)
4. Aggregated EU27 by summing 27 member state columns
5. Computed Rest of World as World total minus the four named regions
6. Filtered to 1959--2023 to match the original chart's timespan
7. Pivoted to long format and computed % share and year-over-year % change

## 5. Improved Visualization

### 5.1 Colour Palette and Theme

```{r}
#| label: setup-theme
# Okabe-Ito colorblind-friendly palette (subset)
region_colors <- c(
  "China" = "#E69F00",
  "United States" = "#56B4E9",
  "European Union" = "#009E73",
  "India" = "#D55E00",
  "Rest of World" = "#999999"
)

theme_slate <- theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

### 5.2 Faceted Line Chart (Small Multiples) -- Total Emissions

```{r}
#| label: fig-faceted-total
#| fig-cap: "Fossil CO2 emissions by region (GtCO2/yr), 1959--2023. Each panel shares a common y-axis for direct comparison."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
# Label positions: latest year value for direct labelling
label_data <- emissions_long |>
  filter(year == max(year))

# Key event annotations
events <- tibble(
  year = c(2015, 2020),
  label = c("Paris\nAgreement", "COVID-19"),
  y_pos = c(Inf, Inf)
)

ggplot(emissions_long, aes(x = year, y = emissions_gtco2, color = region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(
    data = events,
    aes(xintercept = year),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey50",
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = events,
    aes(x = year, y = y_pos, label = label),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    vjust = 1.2,
    hjust = 0.5,
    size = 2.8,
    color = "grey40"
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = label_data,
    aes(label = paste0(round(emissions_gtco2, 1), " Gt")),
    nudge_x = 2,
    size = 3.2,
    direction = "y",
    segment.color = "grey70"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~region, nrow = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_manual(values = region_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1960, 2020, 20)) +
  labs(
    title = "Fossil CO2 Emissions by Region, 1959-2023",
    subtitle = "Each panel shows one region on its own baseline for accurate trend reading",
    x = NULL,
    y = "GtCO2 / year"
  ) +
  theme_slate
```

### 5.3 % Share of Global Emissions

```{r}
#| label: fig-pct-share
#| fig-cap: "Each region's share of global fossil CO2 emissions (%), 1959--2023."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5
label_pct <- emissions_long |>
  filter(year == max(year))

ggplot(emissions_long, aes(x = year, y = pct_share, color = region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = label_pct,
    aes(label = paste0(region, "\n", round(pct_share, 1), "%")),
    nudge_x = 3,
    size = 3.2,
    direction = "y",
    segment.color = "grey70"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = region_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1960, 2020, 10)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Share of Global Fossil CO2 Emissions by Region",
    subtitle = "How each region's contribution has shifted over 65 years",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of global emissions"
  ) +
  theme_slate
```

### 5.4 Sparkline Summary Table

```{r}
#| label: sparkline-table
library(gt)
library(gtExtras)

summary_stats <- emissions_long |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(
    trend = list(emissions_gtco2),
    latest = round(last(emissions_gtco2), 2),
    share = paste0(round(last(pct_share), 1), "%"),
    change_5yr = round(
      (last(emissions_gtco2) - nth(emissions_gtco2, -5)) /
        nth(emissions_gtco2, -5) *
        100,
      1
    ),
    peak_year = year[which.max(emissions_gtco2)],
    peak = round(max(emissions_gtco2), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(latest))

summary_stats |>
  gt() |>
  gt_plt_sparkline(
    trend,
    same_limit = FALSE,
    type = "shaded",
    fig_dim = c(15, 40),
    palette = c("black", "black", "grey80", "grey80", "grey90")
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    region = "Region",
    trend = "Emissions Trend (1959-2023)",
    latest = "GtCO2 (2023)",
    share = "% Share",
    change_5yr = "5yr Change (%)",
    peak_year = "Peak Year",
    peak = "Peak GtCO2"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Fossil CO2 Emissions by Region",
    subtitle = "Summary with embedded sparklines (1959-2023)"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(latest, peak), decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = change_5yr, decimals = 1, force_sign = TRUE) |>
  tab_source_note("Data: Global Carbon Project, GCB 2024") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  )
```

## 6. Key Findings

1. **China** overtook the United States as the largest emitter around 2006 and has continued to grow rapidly, reaching `r summary_stats$latest[summary_stats$region == "China"]` GtCO2 in 2023.

2. **The United States** and **European Union** show declining trends, with the EU peaking in the 1970s--1980s.

3. **India's** emissions are growing but remain far smaller in absolute terms.

4. **Rest of World** accounts for a substantial and growing share, highlighting that emissions are a global challenge, not confined to a few major economies.

## 7. References

- Friedlingstein, P., et al. (2024). Global Carbon Budget 2024. *Earth System Science Data*, 16, 1--102. doi:10.5194/essd-16-1-2024
- Global Carbon Project. (2024). National Fossil Carbon Emissions 2024 v1.0. Retrieved from [globalcarbonbudget.org](https://globalcarbonbudget.org/carbonbudget)
- Carbon Brief. (2024). Global CO2 emissions from fossil fuels by region, 1959--2024. Retrieved from [carbonbrief.org](https://www.carbonbrief.org)
- United Nations. (2024). World Population Prospects 2024. Retrieved from [population.un.org/wpp](https://population.un.org/wpp/downloads)
